title: Selecting a gilt for breeding
subtitle: What to look for before keeping a female pig back from slaughter.
excerpt: Not every female pig from a litter is worth breeding from. A gilt intended for the breeding herd needs more teats than the average, a sound enough structure to carry multiple pregnancies, and a temperament that will allow easy handling at farrowing. Selecting for these traits takes twenty minutes and saves months of management problems.
sourceNotes: AHDB pig breeding and selection guide, ahdb.org.uk. BPEX replacement gilt selection guidelines. DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Pigs.

glossaryTerms.definition: A young female pig that has not yet produced a litter. A gilt is assessed for breeding potential before her first mating, typically at five to six months of age.
The number of functional teats on a sow or gilt, counted along both sides of the underline. A minimum of twelve functional, well-spaced teats is recommended for a breeding female.

content.text: selected from a litter at eight to ten weeks is being assessed on what she will become over three or four years of breeding. The traits that matter are physical: teat count, structural soundness, and vulva development. Temperament matters too, a pig that is difficult to handle at four months will be dangerous to work with during farrowing at eighteen months.

along both sides of the underline while the pig is standing or held by a handler. A minimum of twelve functional teats is the industry standard; fourteen or more is preferable for a gilt expected to rear large litters.

Check that the teats are evenly spaced along the entire length of the underline. A cluster of teats at the front with gaps toward the rear means the rear teats will not be nursed well by piglets. Inverted teats, those that point inward into the body, will not produce milk; count only outward-pointing or flat teats.

Watch the gilt walk toward you and away from you. Front legs should be straight with toes pointing forward. Hind legs should be straight with hocks and stifles (knees) in alignment. Weak pasterns (the joint just above the foot that buckles forward) will worsen with weight and are a reason to cull from the breeding group.
The feet themselves: check for equal-length toes and hard, dark horn. Soft, pale horn and unequal toe length will cause foot problems under the weight of multiple pregnancies.

A gilt with an abnormally small vulva may have difficulty farrowing. The vulva should be of normal size relative to her body, positioned correctly under the tailhead, and without any discharge or swelling. An undershot vulva (angled forward) can predispose to reproductive tract infections.

Handle potential gilts individually. They should calm within thirty seconds of being held against the fence. A gilt that screams persistently, bites, or charges will not improve with age; she will be a farrowing problem at eighteen months and a danger to piglets and stockperson alike.

On a small holding where you will farrow a sow without a dedicated farrowing attendant, temperament is the most important selection criterion. A calm sow that tolerates handling overlies fewer piglets and accepts interventions during a difficult farrowing. A brilliant conformation but difficult temperament is a poor trade.
